subsystem: Tor client-side application-proxy (AP) connection handling. This is connection_ap_handshake_rewrite_and_attach(), the core routine in the SOCKS entry path that takes a freshly received SOCKS request, applies address-map rewrites, classifies the requested destination (regular host/IP, .exit pseudo-host, or .onion hidden service), enforces per-port traffic policy (DNS/IPv4/IPv6/OnionTrafficOnly), rejects internal/reentry/dangerous-port targets, and finally attaches the stream to a circuit. It is the funnel through which every outbound client request passes.
provenance: Identified by the exact Tor internal function name and type vocabulary: entry_connection_t, origin_circuit_t, crypt_path_t, socks_request_t, or_options_t, tor_addr_t, node_t, routerset_t; Tor helper idioms connection_mark_unattached_ap(), safe_str_client(), escaped(), tor_assert()/tor_assert_nonfatal_unreached(), get_options(), rep_hist_note_used_port(); END_STREAM_REASON_* / SOCKS5_HS_* / ADDRMAPSRC_* / SOCKS_COMMAND_* constants; and the .exit / .onion handling. This is src/core/or/connection_edge.c from the Tor source tree. The sample id 'primevul_c_train_2859_vuln' and the 'cwe532' file tag flag it as a sensitive-information-in-logs case.
trust_boundary: The attacker/untrusted input is the SOCKS request produced by whatever local application connected to Tor's SOCKS port: conn->socks_request, above all socks->address (the destination hostname/IP the user or app asked for), socks->port, socks->command, and socks->socks_version. In Tor's threat model socks->address is not merely input to validate for safety — it is itself SENSITIVE: it reveals exactly which host the user is contacting, so any sink that persists or exports it (log files, control-port events) is a disclosure surface. rr.orig_address comes from the rewrite of that same user string. options come from the trusted local configuration; conn/circ/cpath are internal objects.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function receives an entry (AP/SOCKS) connection conn, an optional pre-chosen circuit circ, and an optional crypt_path cpath onto which the stream should be attached. It caches conn->socks_request into socks (the parsed SOCKS request, holding the destination address, port, command and SOCKS version), reads the current configuration via get_options(), derives the generic base connection base_conn from the entry connection, records the wall-clock now for predictive-history bookkeeping, and declares a rewrite_result_t rr that will receive the outcome of the address-map rewrite. socks->address is the primary attacker-influenced field for everything that follows.

rr is zero-initialized so that all of its fields (should_close, end_reason, map_expires, automap, exit_source, orig_address) have defined values, then connection_ap_handshake_rewrite() performs the addressmap/automap/DNS-cache rewrite of socks->address in place and populates rr with the results. After this call socks->address may have been changed from what the user typed, and rr tells the caller how to proceed.

If the rewrite decided the connection is finished (it either already answered the request, e.g. a cached DNS answer, or it errored), the code marks the AP connection unattached with the supplied end reason and returns. It masks rr.end_reason with END_STREAM_REASON_MASK and treats END_STREAM_REASON_DONE as success (return 0) and anything else as failure (return -1). This is the early-exit path when no circuit attachment is needed.

The three rewrite outputs that the rest of the function consumes are hoisted into locals: map_expires (when the addressmap entry that produced this address expires, used when replying to RESOLVE), automap (whether the address was produced by the automap mechanism, which must never coexist with a user-supplied .exit), and exit_source (the provenance of any .exit component, used to reject .exit addresses that came from untrusted sources like AUTOMAP/DNS).

parse_extended_hostname() classifies socks->address into a hostname_type_t (regular host, EXIT_HOSTNAME, ONION_V2/V3_HOSTNAME, BAD_HOSTNAME) and, as a side effect, strips a trailing .exit suffix in place. If parsing fails, the code emits a SOCKS_BAD_HOSTNAME control-port status event carrying escaped(socks->address) — the raw requested hostname, only C-escaped, NOT scrubbed by safe-logging — sets the SOCKS5 extended error code when the type is BAD_HOSTNAME, marks the connection closed with END_STREAM_REASON_TORPROTOCOL, and returns -1. This is the first of several control events that carry the user's destination string.

When the address is a .exit pseudo-hostname, the code selects which router-exclusion set applies: with StrictNodes on it uses the combined ExcludeExitNodesUnion_, otherwise just ExcludeExitNodes. node is the relay that the .exit name resolves to; it starts NULL and MUST be non-NULL by the time the exit block finishes or the request is rejected.

These blocks reject .exit addresses whose provenance is untrusted. A .exit derived from an AUTOMAP entry came straight from a user and is unsafe, so it is refused; likewise a .exit whose source is ADDRMAPSRC_DNS or ADDRMAPSRC_NONE (lines 76-86) is treated as impossible and refused. Note the deliberate asymmetry of the two log sinks: the plain-text log uses safe_str_client(socks->address) (scrubbed when SafeLogging is enabled) while the control event uses escaped(socks->address) (not scrubbed). tor_assert_nonfatal_unreached() records a soft bug marker without aborting.

A hard tor_assert(!automap) enforces that an automapped address can never reach the .exit path (a violated assertion aborts the process). The code then locates the last '.' in socks->address with strrchr. If found and the character after it is non-NUL, the substring after the dot is the exit relay nickname: it is duplicated into conn->chosen_exit_name, looked up with node_get_by_nickname() (returning a borrowed node_t*), and if the .exit came from a TRACKEXIT addressmap the retry counter is primed to TRACKHOSTEXITS_RETRIES. Finally *s = 0 truncates socks->address at the dot so the remaining destination is (stuff).

If the trailing '.' had nothing after it (a (stuff)..exit form) the request is malformed and refused (again logging scrubbed to file but escaped to the control event). If there was no '.' at all, the whole string is a bare relay name such as foo.exit: the whole address becomes the chosen exit name and is looked up; if the relay is known, socks->address is cleared and overwritten with the relay's own address string. The bound passed to node_get_address_string() is sizeof(socks->address), which is only correct if address is a fixed-size in-struct char array rather than a pointer.

After the .exit name is resolved, the code enforces that the chosen exit actually exists (node non-NULL) and that it is not a member of the exclusion set computed earlier. Both failures log a scrubbed message and refuse the request. These are the safety gates that stop a client from pinning traffic to an unknown or forbidden relay.

The large non-onion branch handles ordinary hostnames and IP literals. It first runs address_is_invalid_destination() to reject addresses containing illegal characters; on rejection it again emits both a control event with escaped(socks->address) and a scrubbed log line, then refuses. This is the syntactic validation gate before any policy or resolution decisions.

This block and the following else-if chain (lines 191-220) enforce the listener's traffic policy. If the port allows no DNS, no IPv4 and no IPv6 (OnionTrafficOnly), all non-onion requests are refused. Otherwise tor_addr_parse() classifies socks->address: a parse failure (-1) means a hostname needing DNS, which is refused if dns_request is off; an AF_INET literal is refused if ipv4_traffic is off; an AF_INET6 literal is refused if ipv6_traffic is off; any other family hits a nonfatal unreachable marker. All refusals log the address scrubbed with safe_str_client().

For a RESOLVE command, if the address is already an IP literal the resolve is answered immediately: socks->address is restored to the original pre-rewrite string rr.orig_address via strlcpy (again bounded by sizeof(socks->address)), the SOCKS resolved-address reply is sent, and the connection is closed as done. Otherwise it records a predictive-history note. A hard tor_assert(!automap) guards the non-IP path.

The CONNECT branch begins with tor_assert(!automap), rejects port 0, then (lines 254-292) enforces ClientRejectInternalAddresses: unless the request uses begindir, has a chosen exit, or a circuit is supplied, it refuses connections whose target is a local/internal address (tor_addr_hostname_is_local or tor_addr_is_internal). Two rate-limited warning paths (transparent-proxy loop warning at 266-276 and ordinary private-address warning at 278-287) use static ratelim_t limiters and log the address with safe_str_client(). All these refusals are scrubbed in the log file.

For literal-IP CONNECT targets, the code re-parses the address and applies fine-grained family/version policy: reject if the address family is disallowed by the listener config, reject IPv6 over SOCKS4, reject any SOCKS4 request when IPv4 is forbidden, and otherwise narrow conn->entry_cfg so the exit is told which single family we accept. It then (lines 345-355) refuses connections to known relay/authority OR/Dir ports when network reentry is disallowed (the 'Tor over Tor' guard), logging the address scrubbed.

Still in CONNECT, SOCKS4 forces ipv6_traffic off. Then, when there is no begindir, no chosen exit and no supplied circuit, it looks for an exact exit-enclave relay for the destination and, if found, pins the chosen exit to that relay's hex identity digest (via hex_str(r->identity, DIGEST_LEN)). It also runs consider_plaintext_ports() to warn/reject dangerous ports and records the used port for prediction. The RESOLVE_PTR branch (397-399) needs no extra processing, and a fragile-assert catches any other command.

Once the non-onion request has survived all checks, the connection state moves to AP_CONN_STATE_CIRCUIT_WAIT and the stream is attached: to the caller-supplied circ/cpath if present, otherwise queued as pending for the next attach cycle. A negative return from the attach path marks the connection with END_STREAM_REASON_CANT_ATTACH (if not already closed) and returns -1; success returns 0.

The final else handles .onion addresses. v2 onions are no longer supported: they are refused with a protocol warning, a SOCKS_BAD_HOSTNAME control event carrying escaped(socks->address), and the SOCKS5_HS_BAD_ADDRESS extended error. Otherwise the type must be ONION_V3_HOSTNAME (asserted, along with !automap) and the request is handed to connection_ap_handle_onion(). The trailing return 0 at line 458 is unreachable and only present to satisfy the compiler.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the CWE-532 surface the sample is built around. Throughout the function the plain-text log sinks are carefully scrubbed with safe_str_client(socks->address) (e.g. lines 66, 81, 111, 133, 163, 184, 198, 206, 214, 273, 285, 352), which under SafeLogging replaces the sensitive destination with [scrubbed]. But every SOCKS_BAD_HOSTNAME control-status event (this line and the copies at 67-68, 82-83, 112-113, 159-160, 443-444) passes escaped(socks->address), which only C-escapes control characters and does NOT honor safe-logging. In Tor's threat model socks->address is exactly the piece of information that must not leak: it identifies the site the user is contacting. Any controller that subscribes to STATUS_CLIENT events, or any operator whose control-event stream is echoed/persisted to disk, thereby records the user's real requested hostnames — a strong deanonymization/tracking signal that survives even when SafeLogging 1 is set for the file logs. The invariant the implementation must uphold is that client destination identifiers reaching a persistable sink are scrubbed with safe-logging semantics, or that the operator explicitly consents that control events are trusted. Confirm whether escaped() applies any SafeLogging filtering (it does not) and whether these STATUS_CLIENT events can reach a log/audit file.

This write into socks->address is bounded by sizeof(socks->address). That bound is only correct if address is an in-struct fixed-size character array (in Tor it is char address[MAX_SOCKS_ADDR_LEN]); if socks_request_t were ever refactored so that address is a char* pointer, sizeof would evaluate to the pointer width (4/8) rather than the buffer capacity, and node_get_address_string() would either truncate to a few bytes or, if it treats the size as the destination capacity, write past a smaller allocation — a classic CWE-467/CWE-787 mistake. The same pattern reappears at line 230 (strlcpy(socks->address, rr.orig_address, sizeof(socks->address))). Latent: the shown code is safe against today's struct definition; the contract to confirm is that address remains a fixed-size array member and that MAX_SOCKS_ADDR_LEN is large enough for every node_get_address_string() / rr.orig_address value so no security-relevant truncation of the destination occurs.

The function relies on several hard tor_assert() invariants over attacker-influenced state: !automap here on the .exit path, and again at lines 238 and 243 on the RESOLVE/CONNECT paths, plus tor_assert(addresstype == ONION_V3_HOSTNAME) at line 453. tor_assert aborts the whole Tor process on failure. These are correctness invariants that upstream code (the rewrite and parse_extended_hostname) is supposed to guarantee — an automapped address should never carry a .exit and a hidden-service address should be v3 by the time we reach line 453. If any upstream change let a crafted address violate one of these assumptions, the assertion becomes a remotely-influenced abort (CWE-617 reachable assertion / denial of service) rather than a graceful refusal. Confirm that no attacker-reachable rewrite path can produce automap=1 together with a .exit/RESOLVE/CONNECT address, and that the v3 assertion cannot be reached for a non-v3 onion type.

node_get_by_nickname() returns a BORROWED const node_t* that points into the global nodelist; node is then used well after this point (routerset_contains_node at 138, and read implicitly through the flow). Between the lookup and its uses no network event loop iteration runs in this function, so the nodelist is not expected to be rebuilt underneath it, but this is the pointer-lifetime contract to keep in mind: if any called helper here (e.g. node_get_address_string, routerset_contains_node) could trigger a nodelist reload or free, node would dangle (CWE-416). Dismissed as a live bug in the shown synchronous flow, but noted as the lifetime invariant the implementation must preserve: node must not outlive the nodelist generation it was fetched from.

parse_extended_hostname() and the earlier connection_ap_handshake_rewrite() both mutate socks->address in place based on fully attacker-controlled input, and the classification result (addresstype) then steers the entire control flow — including which trust gates run. The security of everything downstream depends on this classifier being exhaustive and non-spoofable: e.g. a string that parses as a regular hostname but is actually a hidden-service/.exit form, or vice versa, would route the request through the wrong policy checks. This is not a memory bug but the central input-validation contract (CWE-20): confirm that parse_extended_hostname cannot be tricked into misclassifying an onion/.exit address as a plain hostname (which would bypass the onion/enclave handling) or leave socks->address in a state the later strrchr/strlcpy logic mishandles.

summary: This is Tor's connection_ap_handshake_rewrite_and_attach(), the SOCKS request funnel; the destination string socks->address is both attacker-influenced and intrinsically sensitive. The one concrete issue is CWE-532: SOCKS_BAD_HOSTNAME control-status events emit escaped(socks->address), which — unlike the safe_str_client() used on every plain-text log line — is NOT scrubbed by SafeLogging, so the user's real requested hostnames can leak through the control-event channel. The remaining risks are latent contracts (sizeof-bounded writes into socks->address, hard asserts over rewrite-derived state, borrowed node pointer lifetime, and trust in the in-place hostname classifier) rather than bugs visible in this code.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Internal entry point; parameters are trusted internal objects except socks_request reachable via conn — covered by the socks->address anchors.
Carries socks->address, the attacker-influenced and sensitive destination string that drives the CWE-532 disclosure and all downstream string handling.
Local trusted configuration, not attacker-controlled.
Zero-initialized before use; sizeof over a value, not a pointer — no bound bug.
Simple flag/enum handling with defined values; no memory or overflow contract.
automap is enforced via hard tor_asserts over attacker-influenced rewrite output (reachable-assert contract).
In-place mutation and classification of untrusted input steers all downstream trust gates — CWE-20 misclassification contract.
escaped() is not SafeLogging-scrubbed, unlike the safe_str_client() log sinks — CWE-532 sensitive-hostname disclosure.
Constant assignment to a status field; no security contract.
Trusted config selection; the enforcement itself is a safety gate, not a vulnerability surface.
These are the refusal gates that protect the .exit path; they reduce risk rather than create it.
NUL-terminated string handled with strrchr and offset dup; s+1 is in-bounds when s[1] != 0 — no OOB.
Returns a borrowed nodelist pointer used later; lifetime/use-after-free contract to preserve (CWE-416, latent).
Constant assignment and in-bounds truncation at a located '.'; no overflow.
sizeof-as-bound is only safe while address is a fixed array; latent CWE-467/787/truncation contract.
Safety enforcement gates; correct null-check before use.
Syntactic validation gate that reduces risk.
Boolean policy checks with scrubbed logging; refusal logic, not a bug surface.
Writes into stack tor_addr_t of fixed size; parser return value checked before use.
Same sizeof-as-bound contract as line 125; strlcpy is safe only while address is a fixed array (grouped with the line-125 finding).
Enum dispatch with a fragile-assert default; no overflow surface.
Hard asserts over rewrite/classifier-derived state; reachable-assertion DoS contract (CWE-617).
Simple rejection of port 0; no contract.
Internal-address rejection gate; the rate-limited logs use safe_str_client — no leak.
Static rate-limit constants; function-local #defines with no untrusted input.
Version-based policy narrowing; no memory contract.
Tor-over-Tor refusal gate; scrubbed logging.
hex_str over a fixed DIGEST_LEN identity buffer; fixed-width, not attacker-sized.
Port advisory/history bookkeeping; no untrusted-size handling.
State transition and attach with checked return value; no direct input surface.
v2 refusal (with the escaped() control event already covered) and hand-off to the onion handler; v3 hand-off is out of scope of this file.

findings.title: Requested destination hostname disclosed unscrubbed via SOCKS_BAD_HOSTNAME control events
findings.bug_class: Insertion of sensitive information into log/event channel (unscrubbed client destination)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0081_cwe532.c (and the control-event/logging implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Any sink that can persist or export a client's requested destination (socks->address) must apply SafeLogging scrubbing (safe_str_client) exactly as the plain-text log lines in this same function do; escaped() alone (C-escaping only) is not sufficient for a sensitive identifier.
findings.failure_mode: For every rejected/unsupported hostname (bad hostname line 36, stale automap 61, impossible .exit source 76, malformed .exit 108, invalid destination 158, v2 onion 438) the code emits a STATUS_CLIENT control event containing escaped(socks->address) — the user's real requested host. Because escaped() does not honor SafeLogging (unlike the safe_str_client() calls on lines 66/81/111/133/163/184/198/206/214/273/285/352), an attacker or observer with access to the control-event stream, or an operator who logs those events, learns exactly which hosts the user tried to reach even with SafeLogging enabled — a deanonymization/traffic-analysis leak.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that escaped() performs no SafeLogging redaction and that STATUS_CLIENT/SOCKS_BAD_HOSTNAME events can be relayed to a persistent log or an untrusted controller; if so, route the sensitive field through safe_str_client() (or gate on SafeLogging) before it reaches the control event.

findings.title: socks->address writes bounded by sizeof(socks->address) — safe only while address is a fixed array
findings.anchor: node_get_address_string(node, socks->address, sizeof(socks->address)) / strlcpy(socks->address, rr.orig_address, sizeof(socks->address))
findings.bug_class: Wrong sizeof operand / buffer size mismatch (latent overflow or truncation)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0081_cwe532.c and the socks_request_t definition (file not provided)
findings.invariant: socks_request_t.address must remain a fixed-size in-struct char array (char address[MAX_SOCKS_ADDR_LEN]) so that sizeof(socks->address) yields the true buffer capacity, and MAX_SOCKS_ADDR_LEN must be large enough for every value node_get_address_string() or rr.orig_address can produce so no security-relevant truncation occurs.
findings.failure_mode: If address were ever changed to a pointer, sizeof(socks->address) would evaluate to the pointer width (4/8 bytes); node_get_address_string() at line 125 and strlcpy() at line 230 would then either silently truncate the destination to a few bytes (routing the stream to the wrong host) or, depending on the callee's use of the size argument, write past a smaller allocation (CWE-787). Even today, if MAX_SOCKS_ADDR_LEN is too small a long relay address string is silently truncated at line 125.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify socks_request_t.address is a fixed char array, that sizeof therefore equals its capacity, and that MAX_SOCKS_ADDR_LEN cannot be exceeded by node_get_address_string()/rr.orig_address in a way that matters for correctness or safety.

findings.title: Hard tor_assert() over rewrite/classifier-derived state can abort on invariant violation
findings.bug_class: Reachable assertion / denial of service on invariant violation
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0081_cwe532.c (invariant guaranteed by connection_ap_handshake_rewrite/parse_extended_hostname, files not provided)
findings.invariant: The rewrite and hostname-classification stages must guarantee that automap==0 whenever control reaches the .exit path (line 88), the RESOLVE non-IP path (line 238) and the CONNECT path (line 243), and that addresstype is exactly ONION_V3_HOSTNAME when line 453 is reached; these hard asserts abort the process if the guarantee fails.
findings.failure_mode: tor_assert() calls abort() on failure. The asserted conditions are derived from attacker-influenced input processed by the rewrite/classifier. Today the upstream logic enforces them, but any change that let a crafted address arrive with automap set on these paths, or as a non-v3 onion at line 453, would convert a would-be graceful refusal into a remotely-influenced crash (client DoS).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no attacker-reachable rewrite/parse path can deliver automap==1 to lines 88/238/243, and that addresstype at line 453 can only be ONION_V3_HOSTNAME after the ONION_V2 branch has already returned.

audit_checklist: Verify escaped() applies no SafeLogging redaction and that SOCKS_BAD_HOSTNAME/STATUS_CLIENT control events cannot leak socks->address to a persistent log or untrusted controller; scrub the destination on that channel if they can.

Confirm socks_request_t.address is a fixed-size char array so sizeof(socks->address) at lines 125 and 230 is the real capacity, and that MAX_SOCKS_ADDR_LEN never forces a security-relevant truncation of node_get_address_string()/rr.orig_address output.

Prove the automap==0 and addresstype==ONION_V3_HOSTNAME invariants asserted at lines 88, 238, 243 and 453 cannot be violated by any attacker-shaped address, so the hard tor_asserts cannot become a remote abort.

Check the lifetime of the borrowed node from node_get_by_nickname() (lines 101/122): ensure no helper called before routerset_contains_node()/node use can rebuild or free the nodelist, which would dangle node (CWE-416).
Audit parse_extended_hostname() and connection_ap_handshake_rewrite() to ensure a crafted address cannot be misclassified so it bypasses the onion/.exit/internal-address trust gates (CWE-20).

Confirm every remaining log sink that prints socks->address uses safe_str_client() (lines 66/81/111/133/163/184/198/206/214/273/285/352) and that none was accidentally switched to a raw %s or escaped().
